Passkeys
A sane talk
A standard for
password-less
logins
•No more remembering passwords
•No more accidentally entering your
password into a website linked in a very
legitimate email
•No more insecure server side password
storage
•Literally couldn't give it away if you wanted
to
•No need to transmit sensitive cryptographic
keys (like passwords, even if hashed).
bUt I jUsT
ReMEMbeR aLl
mY
paSSwORds
You don't and always forget them
or
You use the same password for
everything
(not) FUN PASSWORD FACTS
-69% of the time, Gen Z uses a variation of a single password. (not a joke)
-More than 6 in 10 people admit to reusing passwords.
-96% of the most common passwords can be cracked by hacking tools in less than one
second.
-3% of people use the exact same password for all of their accounts.
Source: https://us.norton.com/blog/privacy/password-statistics
Alright whats a Passkey
Passkeys
/ˈpasˌkēs/
noun
Based on FIDO standards, passkeys are a replacement for passwords that provide faster,
easier, and more secure sign-ins to websites and apps across a user’s devices. Unlike
passwords, passkeys are always strong and phishing-resistant.
Passkeys simplify account registration for apps and websites, are easy to use, work
across most of a user’s devices, and even work on other devices within physical proximity.
How Does it Work
-Public-key cryptography
-On creation your device generates a key pair (public key and private key), the public key is sent
to the server and the private key is stored securely on-device (or in an end-to-end encrypted
cloud manager like Apple Keychain or Google Password Manager).
-To authenticate, the server sends a challenge to your authenticator (device) which can only be
solved by your private key, once done only the response is sent back to the server. Meaning
your private key never leaves your device.
-Built in 2FA, you will always need a device or service to act as the authenticator, thus you have a
second factor.
-Part of the Passkey challenge includes the domain the response is sent to, it's very hard (maybe
impossbile?) to send a valid response back to the wrong target.
-Same standard used by the latest hardware authentication devices like the Yubikey.
What if I lose my Authenticator?
-You're probably fine
-Services like Apple Keychain and Google Password Manager allow you to recover your
keys usually with the same lock that was used on the device it was created on. (FaceID,
fingerprint, screen-lock etc.)
Advantages
Disadvantages
-No more "what's my password
again?"
-No more trusting that a server isn't
storing your password in plaintext (or
poorly hashed)
-Can't accidentally enter your
password (or passkey) in
facebok.com instead of
facebook.com
-Not susceptible to a man in the
middle attack, someone monitoring
your traffic can't see anything
important even if the traffic is
unencrypted
TLDR
-Not many services allow you to
exclusively use Passkeys, still
requiring passwords or 2FA as
backup.
-Not great for technological equality,
while the latest devices and services
might use passkeys, older devices
and services outside of the West
might be left behind or
disadvantaged.
-A real password-less future is a long
way away
But I use a Password Manager???
-Great! It probably has added or will add support for Passkeys, and
generally these act on the same principles as a hardware-specific
service.
-Using a password manager and transitioning accounts to
Passkeys is a great reasonably secure way to move away from
passwords.
TLDR: TLDR:
-Passwords bad
-Passkeys good
Find out where you can start using
passkeys at passkeys.directory
